text:
giving myself thirty minutes to thread every day as a process for inkuary seemed like a good way to build momentum for a writing practice this month, but for the first day i kinda want to give myself more room here and do a long post instead.

already i’m noticing how i’m slowly typing as i cross the character limit. a question of: “what’s worth leaving people a snippet of so i can get people to continue reading?” but already i’ve edited the previous 280 characters and tweaked the configurations and phrasing before i proceed. writing on twitter does seem to bring the craftsman out of me, but having the character constraint baked into the logic to the platform, it has now ended up being more of an imagined limit with the premium membership. funny enough i only really bought it to upload longer videos when i was recording ukulele covers. check my highlights tab if you want to check those out.

i spent about 8 minutes editing that previous paragraph before writing the current paragraph you are reading right now. i’m thinking of going through this one as a full pass without editing in real time. there’s a bit of a flow that i want to get more situated with on here that will help me contrast with thinking too hard about what to write or getting stuck. really it’s a decision matrix of questions that i have yet to really tune. so what am i practicing here? nimbleness for one. how far can i stay focused on communicating what’s on my mind with the minimal amount of latency between thought and expression? how well can i orient between completing a thought and following the next thought that comes to mind? is this a velocity that can be adjusted based on my environment? how much can i really trust being able to take leaps rather than going step by step?

ahh you know what the thing that is so hard for me about thinking systematically? it’s too goddamn slow. taking my time to think things through is uncomfortable for me, because i never really know when something is really done being thought through. it’s quite apt to describe my working process as oscillating between going so fast just to get the job done, or so slow that nothing will get done at all. so really what i want to get more practice with is finding the *appropriate tempo* for thinking. how can i keep the beats between thoughts consistent and tractable for other people to follow along with me? that is a surprising skill issue that i would not have considered before going into this long post, so this feels like a worthwhile tactic. i was about to go to bed without getting any writing done, so feeling glad to have stuck it out. alright, it’s 10:08pm and this feels like a natural pace to stop. plus my mobile keyboard is starting to lag heavy on me, so i will come back here tomorrow. gg work gn yall

text:
okay @evodevotee i'm following your advice and doing my inkuary daily longpost on our conversation in the hopes of rejecting the nebulousness of an audience uncaptured void and accepting this place more like one large group chat, it was honestly so clutch to get ur feedback on my thoughts re why i've been so enamored with Spell of the Sensuous by David Abram (even though i still need to actually finish the book lmao) because the book actively demonstrates what i aim for with my writing style on twitter which gestures toward a continual return to hearing what you read, as an intentional error-corrective mechanism for the value of literacy *on the basis of orality*

bringing up the example of how the KiDs ThEsE dAyS are learning how to read by guessing what the next word is rather than sounding out the word letter by letter, we're forgetting the phonetic basis on which language, as a human invention, comes from. and sure, you can argue for a better neuroplastic fit when talking with large language models but idk man (oh gosh but even now my writing of the acronym *idk* ended up reading in my mind's ear as "eye dunno" and that might end up being lost in translation but i digress, i'm writing for clarity here) maximizing for human throughput of information just ends up becoming the dominant use case for the written word, and the bar for how much a person needs to know how to read to get up to speed just ends up becoming so daunting.

maybe i'm being too pedantic in believing that a lot of meaning in language that matters actually comes with the poetics of naming and treating it as a legitimate artform, which i don't think is appreciated as much anymore. i think a lot about the linnean form of taxonomic classification has roots in other languages like latin or germanic (and maybe other languages??), but contrast that with chemical compounds having systematic names that grazes past my ears like 3,4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine, (and even then i only picked that example because MF DOOM knew how to make it sound good, check out All Outta Ale).

but okay in that previous paragraph, you can see how people adept in language are able to bring the words back down into something more digestible for the body when appropriately contextualized, so i'm maybe more sensitive to just how much context is needed to even scaffold for these senses to be activated again, let alone left alone to our own devices. the terminally onliner within me will prolly decry this as the beta shinji meme crouched down and facepalmed at his folding chair, surrounded by the unpalateable wordslop that is circulating throughout whatever this week's discourse kicked up. but i'm legitimately less worried about the overwhelm of new words to learn, and more concerned about the deadening of our human faculty to resonate with what is being communicated through language in and of itself. i think a lot about @yoltartar's refactoring of the Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis: "which says that language shapes what concepts we’re able to think about. I think a more accurate description might be that language influences what sensations we treat as important or unimportant."

based on how we have chosen to write and read, we've treated our eyes to be more important than our ears. and that saddens me.

anyways it's getting late and i want to go to bed before midnight and i've been working on this for about... an hour, i think? we'll see how these thoughts land tomorrow, but thank you for listening to me today and appreciate your attention friend

text:
lmao @christineist cant believe you invited me to this rad mediterranean club to get some writing done even though its saturday night and people are here to get classy crunk, but i think we're def adding to the vibes locked into our laptops and wineglasses, and its so hype to see u writing in google docs for your first blogpost this year, seeing your thinking in tables when substack could never really just makes me bullish in gdocs as the future in bespoke online publishing

although i empathise with substock as a newsletter platform that primarily keeps the lights on from online subscriptions, there's something natural that comes with the document model that gdocs is able to capture about how writing is naturally presented (maybe due to the physical referent of A4 paper??) versus an email that has probably has to deal with limited formatting restrictions (like worse than markdown? come on) so you can't have the elaborate tables that groove your mind Meaningness-style that is simple enough to make with a dropdown option.

but i think what is continuously underrated for gdocs is the distribution mechanism that people just casually take for granted. it reminds me a lot like youtube where you have basically unlimited storage for video content and all you really have to do is just hit record and press upload and you can just casually build a corpus of content. it's honestly really inspiring to see you casually accumulate your vlogs and writing in your crazy lists of lists that is basically way better than a personal website as a reminder that you really can just do things before ai can just do things for us now. 

honestly kinda hung up on the fact that porting your work out of google docs ended up being so difficult, but it makes sense, to each medium their own, and even though the newsletter place is becoming a place for serious writers to surf the strange attractor with their grand theories and bullet points for life, having a lil garage of your private collection of soon to be pdf minifestos or a sea of blue hyperlinks to sift through feels more fitting as a place to grow and expand one’s thinking that more comfortably fits with the ethos of the internet. this used to be a place for Document Object Models before we made everything run bootstrapped by javascript GODDAMNIT

anyways it’s almost midnight i’m still buzzed from my cocktail and i need to press publish like you said im legit plopped on my couch now, so thanks for listening to your jet lag so we could leave early and now i can fall asleep BUT HOPEFULLY ON MY BED GN

text:
following up here i actually woke up thinking about perfection after talking with you about how much effort it takes to make something *spotless*, and one thing i really want to develop is a better sense for completion. i’m sooo bottlenecked by making the idea perfectly worded in my head before notating it down, when really it’s more like a process that has to take place when you actually start to write

david lynch has a tag that goes “write the idea in full” and his phrasing evokes a kind of largeness that isn’t really captured when you just focus on what words makes sense in your head, it’s really more of the image or felt sense that surrounds it and i honestly feel kinda stupid in believing that the way those words read in your head will end up landing the exact same way when you see them outside of you, but it’s kinda like dropping food coloring in water, the droplet ends up diffusing through wherever it lands, so of course the flow is going to change

so maybe that sense of completion is when the water looks like the kind of tint and texture that is held in your mind, which will not match the intensity of the shade of the droplet that falls into the fluid; understanding that there’s even a distinction like that at all gives me a sigh of relief since it feels more grounded in reality than “drop perfect words onto page and you’re done”. that’s not really thinking by writing. that belief hides the work that has gone through the wringer of spotlessness in achieving shared understanding across many minds and places, by continually asking the question: “does this look the same to you as it does to me?”

hey @tasshinfogleman you blogged a good post yesterday on "the skill of asking for life-changing favors", it made me stop in my tracks to really consider what kind of favor i could ask that'd actually change my life, so today's inkuary daily is my way to start practicing that and at the moment i'd like to ask you personally about alternative payment options to take part in your Corpus Builder program, potentially a deposit or milestone based arrangement. i can't currently afford paying the full price upfront, and your help can make our potential collaboration much more feasible

i've built up a surprising amount of momentum over the past few days writing these longer paragraphed essays here on twitter, as the format gives me much more room to expound on ideas that required longer articulation, while framed within the social nature of twitter, along with the shared context that i've built up with a lot of my mutuals on the platform. on top of inkuary being a really supportive peer infrastructure that has brought out more of me than i had believed myself to possess, i find that my bottlenecks at this point is more on personal infrastructure. your digital productivity guide has been useful in climbing the skill tree for organizing my life (and we know how much mileage one can get out of a good SOP), but i find myself needing a project to *really* tie all those skills together and creating a body of work is such a natural fit to make these things happen

while the rest of inkuary is happening, i feel like getting  help in diagnosing the blind spots and difficult feelings that come with shifting from a spontaneous to a more systematic style of making content will supercharge what is already such a generative container. and who knows what's going to happen? ,,but if there are any reservations on your part no hard feelings, this is just calling in a favor after all and i'm just grateful to keep practicing and put in the reps at this point. anyways, wishing you well and excited to hear from you soon

ayo @strangestloop hope you don't mind me publicly collabing on your questions about games for my inkuary daily, some of my distinct memories are gaming related that i haven't covered in full detail and never really get the chance to nerd out so imma just talk about my time playing the witness by jonathan blow which i know you're a big fan of,

it was my junior year of high school when i was immediately enamored by the teaser trailer for the game; a sprawling island littered with these maze panels that started out simple then expanded into deep mechanical interactions, it was a kind of game that seemed to trust their players to learn how to play and figure out its rules through experimentation and discovery. i scoured any kind of promotional material i could find surrounding the game: podcasts talking about the tutorial design and jblow being serious in discussing his philosophy on pedagogy in games, articles detailing the architectural fidelity for the buildings and landmarks on the island to the point of craftsmanship like adding metal brackets onto wooden beams, and even talks given in game developer conferences where he gave his thoughts on the role of video games in education. i admired how much detail and depth him and his development team Thekla put into their little Myst-like of a world.

i distinctly remember booting up the game when it finally released at 2am in the morning On a TUESDAY *During a SCHOOL WEEK*, hands shaking as my screen stared across a hollow tunnel of a tutorial section, getting familiarized with the controls to then walk up the stairs into the island world, with the peak of the mountain framed within view of the door as you step out into the walled up terrace. i had no idea whether to take my time staring at the vines and flowerbeds or rush straight through the maze panels. i ended up doing a bit of both, seeing if there were any hidden passageways or secret tunnels. if anything, i took my time appreciating how easefully the game ramped up in complexity and how much variation there could be in just drawing a line from a dot to a cap.

eventually got out of the lil castle tutorial zone and i sniffed out any off-beaten path or something that looked out of place, and ended up finding a passageway onto the walled walkway where knights would probably do night watch or something, and at the end of the path rewarding a gorgeous overview of the  and the nice paved main path that led out to the rest of the grassy starting area where huh wait, doesn't the end kinda look like the dot thats on the panels what if i clicked on oH mY FUCKING GOD WHAT THE SHIT THIS GAME HAS MORE MAZES THAN JUST 

so when my senioritis kicked in, i sneaked out as much playtime as i could get in during classes that allowed us to use our laptops. while people were grinding out dark souls 3 at 15fps or watching videos on worldstar, i was just in the back navigating through the different zones, like the marble quarry and the fall treehouses. the sun desert area was the most painful to go through, i probably spent a week in my guam studies class just trying to find the right reflections in the water for the puzzles and got so sucked into finding the right angle that i tilted my screen and moved my head around with my camera thinking that any additional angling can get the light just right when my eyes darted toward my teacher as he stared back at me, neck craned over my keyboard and cheek inches away from my screen. "whatever it is that you're doing, you can do it later".

at some point i plan on replaying it with refreshed eyes as the ten year anniversary is approaching, but i am honestly so hyped for his new game Order of the Sinking Star that's supposed to come out sometime in 2026 (fingers crossed) which is why the Witness has been on my mind lately. he cooked up an entirely new game engine AND programming language just to make this new game, no wonder why this took ten years to come out like holy fuck anyways that's enough nerdy nostalgia for today thanks for the prompt loopy gn

text:
haha @ForecastFire at some point i rly gotta call these inkuary dailies "supertweets", you dropped a banger of a handle and am gladly stealing it; was about to write to another friend of mine today but your 30 minute cybernetic lore drop got stuck in my head and i need to process like holy fuck i didn't believe you when you told me that george spencer brown was lovers with wittgenstein during his "queer phase" but your youtube video got to paint such a vivid picture of the guy that it makes sense that someone like him would write Laws of Form, by gawd i wish he would have shared his proof on the twin prime conjecture AND the goldbach conjecture but like usual everyones making opps out of each other in the mathematics community where folks end up fighting to the death (pour one out for our boi Galois)

yknow since being your friend i'm surprised how approachable cybernetics is as a topic in our conversations especially when you get to hear the lives of these people involved in the topics of research, which feels so removed from the aesthetic capture that comes out of people associating the field with images of carbosilica humanoids or state automata hypersigils when really it just started with folks messing around with control systems and i mean today i found out that the word itself literally comes from a greek word for "steering a ship" (which is also where we get kubernetes?? like wtfff) and tbh all the stuff i've read about ooda loops and feedback mechanisms start to make a bit more sense with this context in mind once i got closer to the ideas themselves

speaking of steering ships, i kinda got sidetracked going over my notes for my reading of Stubborn Attachments by Tyler Cowen which i've enjoyed so far just on the surprisingly humane treatment of measuring wealth being accounted for beyond gdp, like accounting for leisure time and environmental health increasing with economic wealth, what i found interesting was when he mentioned “...the wealth of the West helped to enable the export miracles of the East Asian economies” and got myself thinking about how far we've come from the Silk Road routes and how the gravity of specialization and trade had shifted primarily westward up until today, which got me realising just how crazy of an invention sailing ships must have been when they dropped like holy fuck portugal legit unlocked a third of the map from the fog of war they must have felt godlike

from there i just have a bunch of open questions like: how isolated were the americas from the rest of the world by comparison? i remember the alaskan land bridge but dang just how much culture was cultivating there? and how were those sailing ships actually constructed? like imagine all of the excess wood and fabric, the sheer amount of material complexity that was needed to bootstrap and build those things hundreds of years ago, oh gosh royalty must of had a lot of wealth on their hands to fund a bunch of crazy expeditions like this

man idk if its just me but there seems to be an air of frontiersmanship that be brewing as a result of these tools just casually making so much more available to us, and its just a matter of having a greater sense of the horizon that you want to pursue, what edge is worth chasing, i can't help but picture the last episode of hunter x hunter where gon learns about the dark continent and how vast the world truly is at the top of a sapling of a world tree, just being at the beginning of infinity means that you're always at the point of increasing exponentiation, so the feeling of vertigo is something that one must get used to and integrate in one's daily life or you end up nauseated and in vertigo. but at the same time, how do you balance this with a sense of healthy appreciation of so much great things happening around you every day if it ends up being part of the background?

if you ever take a look at my phone screen, or even my twitter banner, there's an image that i've returned to in remembering for myself of what a felt sense of that balance can be like. it's shown up in moments where i can't help but be humbled by life itself and all i can really say is "huh okay", and just notice in wonder at it all. it's kinda crazy how much has happened so far, in the past year, let alone the past three months,, or even the *past seven days*,,, and this is just another day to end on. thanks for spending the day with me friend and i'll see you tomorrow

text:
there’s a lack of seriousness that i’ve noticed in my writing habit now that the fear of losing my daily writing streak is becoming my primary motivator, rather than stoking the care to share what i find interesting with friends & collaborators, so im tweaking my inkuary daily to diagnose what went wrong and how to move forward

i’ve written and deleted multiple paragraphs trying to think through and break down what’s going on but i think im fighting against a lack of energy and a lack of inspiration. feeling split in priority has me feeling stuck on what is important. so if there’s a feeling im running away from that’s causing this stuckness, it really is confronting fear from being potentially rejected, which is such a fucken perfectionist trap man;

honestly it feels kinda futile to even write about it cuz this looks so obvious in hindsight, but really what i feel this come down to is that i don’t feel like myself at the moment. which okay writing actually helps me remember. a lot of writers who treat it as a thinking process say that “they write to forget”, so maybe this is more of “writing as remembrance”, an invocation that is really more to refresh my attention toward what’s being recalled, to be treated as something held more continuously. you know since more people are using words to maximally deliver information in increasingly higher feedback loops rather than to reflect and ponder, no wonder why i feel so hesitant to even write this late with such a rushed approach in mind because i was so distracted on keeping up the streak that i forgot why this practice was important to me to devote all this time and energy in the first place.

okay this feels like enough, i’ll see you tomorrow, thanks for reading.

text:
lmao @tasshinfogleman think i'm just gonna knock this inkuary daily out right now while the inspo is fresh to scrappily talk about the "life changing skill of pre-requisite trees" after seeing you plan out your financial flourishing; there's some key moves here you do that unlocks a felt sense of growing clarity toward your Objective while giving yourself enough room to orient around a complicated problem

i noticed that you'd started out with milestones that are both tangible with metrics and grounded in outcomes which seem like external constraints that foothold your progress toward the goal, but what feels particularly resonant to me is the emotional milestone that seemed to emerge _after_ those steps were charted out. it gave the tree a sense of momentum for why you'd needed to make this in the first place, it became personal and real, despite taking such a logical approach to understanding the problem, and seeing that suprised me to be like yea man there's room for *BOTH* fuck it breh emotions are outcomes too

really the magic is in having the information organized as a dependency graph of subproblems that externalizes the relationships as a logical flow, which words really don't have to linearly hold anymore with "ANDs" & "ORs", so there's room to really visualize it all out. i was impressed with the intuition you developed after making so much of these that i just see you swiftly link together which Milestones feed into which, surface any potential blockages to Overcome, refactor any dependencies that are redundant, and collate groups of lines together into labeled branches. it was especially satisfying to see the software handling the layout *on its own* so you can just focus on the ordering and connections, Flying Logic definitely lives up to its name

and even though it's not pictured, having the option to label just "Feelings" as a subproblem to "Overcome" helps take emotions seriously rather than literally, and what's really cool is that it can end up being an unconsciously flagged subproblem that can be engaged with *while* the pre-req tree is being charted out, which you can then come back to name the feelings in detail after a bit of focusing. that's real work right there and seeing you do it in the middle of organizing the nodes into groups was dope as fuck

but really, seeing the tree laid out from right to left, it's apparent to me that it answers two questions really well: what is the goal? and what can you do right now to get there? getting to a place where you can feel done in recognizing what are the subproblems that feels immediately actionable was amazing to experience with you and oh shiiiit this is basically an emergent todo list that makes tasks and outcomes concrete and you can just put it down and return to it any time when you get more data goddamn this is good stuff bro thank you this was fun

okay yea it feels good to call this out after putting so much pressure on myself to show up here every day and prove that i can keep a consistent writing habit, but there were some days that i legitimately could not press send because i was *resisting against* the ideological weight of Being a Consistently Good Writer, i feel like you can empathise with this given your journalism background, but like damn i chose to do this for fun not because it's my job lmao

speaking of, i lowkey experienced a phase shift today with the Hunt after being so blocked on speccing out any piece of code and realized that i was unconsciously resisting against being The Perfect Candidate, like legit honing onto a single company kinda crippled me into having an all-or-nothing mentality about developing an impressive demo and even just having other companies on the side opened up in other tabs lowered the stakes so much. even just entertaining the prospect of applying to other companies terrified me until i recognized how irrational of a fear of saying no to companies that i don't want to work in as a double bind for receiving rejection, like even having backup options is okay, which makes me lowkey think that going to my dream college altered my perception of tunneling toward entitlement for what i want, while also setting expectations for unconsciously avoiding it at the same time due to my experience being nightmarish which DAMN feels crazy to call out like this at once thank gawwd another thing to sublimate by rejecting jobs as if it's something to coerce myself into like a schoolish frame it's possible to leave any situation with dignity and hit da bricks (also i made another project today instead of what i worked on yesterday, you can also just pivot into what feels alive at any given moment)

anyways yea we're moving through a lot here and writing through it feels like another healthy way of processing the whole thing post bootcamp, i do think that this integration process can be scary to do publicly at times but idgaf anymore man this needs to be fucking done so i can move on and live my life and bloom so thanks crys you're a great friend for throwing that event fr

text:
hey @davidshimel thanks for hosting today's trajectory club it was actually a pretty good session to talk about what i've been up to the past couple of weeks with the mathacademy & inkuary daily combo while at the tech hub, so i'm writing a quick eod to clarify what i'm doing for the Hunt by building (and writing) in public

right now, i got a claude project loaded up with Skiena's "Algorithm Design Manual" and Skycak's "The Math Academy Way" making a spec for Datacademy, which is my scrappy way of foundationally learning data structures and algorithms without grinding leetcode hards (as an aside, surprised how much file contexts they're able to hold, as well as how navigable they are in reading large pdfs). at the end of this, i'm hoping to build out a bespoke version of learning algorithm design with the ux that mathacademy has tuned really well. after making my typing game by taking inspiration from monkeytype, i feel like this will be a cool reverse-engineering challenge that focuses less on juicy interface and game feel and more on system design and cognitive science. at the end of it, i know i'm making a good product if i actually level up for my interview prep while experimenting with all the new agentic coding primitives that are starting to emerge

if i were to envision any blockers, i'd be concerned about the level of content that will need to be generated to make sufficiently gradiented subproblems that are effective and memorable in an actual coding interview, along with creating the knowledge graph from scratch, making non-trivial decisions on how all the dependencies line up. jargon aside, i aim for this project to be usable for people that has the experience with MathAcademy or LeetCodes to get some value going over the basics again cuz even tho its like pulling teeth there's actual good skills in solving these kinds of problems well and i want to get it down to where i don't need to think about it, like doing basic arithmetic in my head. that's what MathAcademy has helped me with, that's what i want out of Datacademy, wish me luck gn

text:
yo @simon_ohler my laptop battery pooped out so my bad for dropping out early but loved catching up with you bro and thanks for reaching out in the replies, also really appreciate you calling out my clarity skill issues, it makes me wanna practice "prefacing the abstract" for today's inkuary daily. i agree that i can give my listeners more of a handle on where i'm going with my words and a lot of that comes from taking my time to slow down and thinking with my body just as much as my mind. i was in the middle of drinking my coffee that morning, so added with the excitement of seeing you after... almost a year?!?! got me pretty fucken hyped ngl hahahaha

just looking back at a couple of instances where you signposted well in our conversation: i appreciated how you explicitly were looking for a scenario that'd resonate with me when you were giving context about your new coaching business, it was a good primer that got me to consider how you were aiming to connect with me with the picture you were painting. a lot of the time when i write, on top of being immediate and conversational, i aim to be matching the form with the content (as commented by my friend shanthi). if anything, those two perspectives land the same to my approach because i'm basically getting you to read my words in the same way that you'd hear me speak, like imagining my voice in your mind's ear.

granted, that's pretty distracting to call out so directly when my aim is to speak and write more clearly, and that clarity comes with conviction and confidence with the way that my ideas are expressed, and being so pedantic about how these words come across ends up disconnecting me from the points that are actually meant to be convey. if anything, that's really what causing me to get "lost in abstraction" when self-consciousness about upholding a particular format starts to becomes a chore, it loses the spark of what the format is meant to hold in the first place

…but it seems like your comments around getting losing the plot while we were talking was less on form and more on content (but correct me if i’m wrong in the replies), and yet this brings us back to taking a pause; whether you’re clear as Laozi or abstract as JPeterson, it’s an infinite good to take time to be precise in terms to know you can be received better. even just taking the time to consider how much we need to know about everything else to understand anything at all, it’s a miracle that we’re able to communicate at all.

idk how we got to reach this point but as always speaking with you brings out the divinity and wisdom that currents through all of us so it was quite fine to chop it up with you, berlin is in good hands, many blessing to you and your loved ones

text:
hey @meatballtimes i gotta talk with you real quick about why i wanna build datacademy cuz a lot of my prep around training in algo design reminds me a lot of what we did with the programming puzzles during week four of the bootcamp and the way mathacademy keeps learning on rails for drilling calc and trig rules in their Math Foundations course is the kind of fine-tuned workout i sorely wished was available for solving array manipulations or graph traversal problems

so i have a feeling that leetcode style problems in the interview process will remain to be a useful skill to screen any potential candidate, since there's no better way to surface a person's ability to think through a solution before touching a live codebase. but tbh it's hard for me to really embrace this fact since i freeze so hard at approaching any of these problems both when i had prior interviews and when we were doing those puzzles. but these aren't any different from the math i'm doing right now in my notebook other than syntax, so what's the difference? i believe that no one has ever   really *taught* me how to think through or break these problems down into scaffolding steps.

there was an anecdote that shook me at the beginning most recent MathAcademy podcast, where Skycak mentioned how college level math is inaccessible to a lot of undergrads due to the amount of implicit scaffolding that's required of students in long problem sets that take hours to complete, so people just "fall off the rails". and in my experience, i found that the magic in keeping students on track with their pedagogy comes from how they break down, interleave, and create dependencies out of all of these micro-skills that can be trained and mastered by students in minutes at a time. they've painstakingly created a gradient for middle and high school level math so smooth that i didn't even notice that these scaffolding steps had become automatic like mental math.

i wish that i had that level of care and attention given to me for really understanding the discrete math that underlies a lot of these interview questions and not treat them as a hoop to jump through. and if i'm already having fun working out these skills with stuff i should have mastered in high school *with no shame whatsoever*, who's to say this can't also be done with the kind of math i should have mastered in college? and fuck it, why can't i make it for myself? what can a russell-shaped data structures and algorithm gradient look like? can other people benefit from this gradient?

these are the kind of questions that are guiding me for this lil side project, and who knows where that kind of thinking will take me, but i figured i shared it with you personally cuz that last eval we did for the 2D robot problem was the most satisfying thrill i felt solving an data modeling assignment2 of an algorithm in my life so far. i want to chase that euphoria again, and have a solid foundation to stand on not by grinding problem, but targeting and strengthening skills, to where it becomes second nature. thanks for reading bro and best of luck with the next cohort

hey @zencephalon i’ve been enjoying your daily writing posts lately. there’s something about your writing style that feels direct and accessible. maybe it’s the short sentences. or rather, it’s how each sentence holds a single idea. regardless, it’s a way of thinking that i’ve begun to appreciate.

sometimes i wonder if that can be a method of improving how i write. i recall how you choose to have no “is” words when you write. it helps make what i read more active in my mind. i can feel greater emphasis on verbs and their direction. and nouns feel sharper. every word counts and serves a purpose. feels clean too. the first paragraph felt easy to write. this one took more effort. did it take a lot of time to train writing like this?

each new sentence gets harder to keep up. this worked my brain harder than i expected. i respect your commitment to this rule. i’ll stop here for this post.

text:
dear @rslantonie i know you’ve been pretty hard on yourself lately, especially as the new year has you busy sowing seeds with math and words and code and all that good stuff; so just thought to set some time aside to really let you know just how much i’ve appreciated your efforts so far and the level of commitment that you’ve shown in sticking to what you value each and every day.

you can grow to be more proud of the person that you’re becoming, and that comes with savoring every single step that you take (and this is key) as you already are. i know how much of the end takes up your attention that sometimes you forget to even begin with what’s right in front of you, or lose your sense of presence as you take the next step. and yet, it’s so interesting how every time you stop caring about what happens, it’s just a matter of remembering yourself.

self-possession isn’t something to take for granted. it’s consequential for every single action, any particular move or potential connection that is out there waiting for you. recognize that what the world wants is *you*, and you are YOURS before anyone or anything else. yea sometimes that’s a lot to ask for and asks a lot of you, but who else can really answer for that?

text:
omg @pee_zombie this is going to sound hella out of left field from our conversation today but it just hit me on the way back home as to how anime as a medium is designed to portray subjective interiority at a higher fidelity compared to other visual forms due to its roots in the comic artistry that mangakas use to engross readers into a character's perspective (this is gonna be quick cuz i need to go to sleep so bear with me lmao

this point actually dovetails really well with another conversation with another friend @the_powertool on how he picked up the nuance of darcy's motivations (from austen's pride and prejudice) by reading into the inner dialogue of his thoughts that the book was able to explicate, which ended up coming out flattened in more live action settings that end up being only implied by social cues and body language. what ends up becoming unconscious signals in a visual medium can be textualized

contrasting this also with telenovelas, the kind of dramatized explications that actors emote take their influence from theater, with exaggerated gestures designed to be telegraphed from greater distances due to stage settings like ampitheaters with a wide field of vision. what ends up being cliche actually had a function: clarity of expression.

but now we have the flatness of our screens basically making any gesture infinitely detailed, so any subtleties can be magnified for greater effect. some slice of life anime actually leverage this quite well, despite public perception being depicted as flashy and stylized. but with manga being flashy and stylized to the point of having multiple paged spreads of reaction images, there's still a lot of room for a reader to get into the mind of a character due to the implied closeness that a book can afford my melding the outer and inner world so effortlessly, with speech and gesture taking up equal space on the page.

i know i'm prolly missing a lot of nuance here with early film pioneers taking the dreamlike nature of cinema to its limits by blending foreground and montage together, but i think there's something to be said with how anime makes it center around character perspective with its heavy use of explication in dialogue that is normally left implied to the image, anyways this is probably getting a bit long at this point and don't want to ramble for too long but you brought up good points and wanted to start crystallizing them here and see if anything resonated, glad to run into you at crystal's hope to see you again soon

text:
Let me tell you about my first love.

We met at school. Not many people liked her.
In fact, she was often ignored, if not avoided.
One day, she'd leave behind some notes for me.
I picked it up, and figured out her strange symbols.
A lot of folks felt off put by this weird little game.
But to me, she just made sense.

I took pride in getting to know her well
As if she let me in on secrets
no one cared enough to listen to
There were times she felt ruthless
if details were missed
If anything, she taught me how
to care about the little things
Once I understood my mistakes
She forgave me, and we continued
to play our little game

At some point, pride got the best of me
as I treated my knowing her
to be absolute
that I can do no wrong
in seeing her as she really is

And yet
her ruthlessness met my pride

She took everything from me
to the point where
i could only bring myself low

to understand her once again
as if for the first time

only then was it clear
what gift she truly wished to give

she left
and yet i see her 
everywhere
i go

how her truth fits with itself
with and without her
and with everything else
